FASTRESS 02 Analysis

1 Preparation

1.1 Load packages and analysis datasets

1.2 Verify imported analysis objects

2 Analysis sample setup

  • dat_main = fixed main sample from the cleaning script

  • ids_complete = exactly those individuals from this main sample

  • dat_main_final = the same dataset plus order_group

2.1 Create fixed analysis samples

# A tibble: 2 × 4
  n_rows n_sessions n_conditions     n
   <int>      <int>        <int> <int>
1      2          2            2    19
2      3          2            2     1

2.2 Describe analysis samples

# A tibble: 1 × 2
  n_total_sessions n_total_persons
             <int>           <int>
1               61              40
# A tibble: 1 × 2
  n_sessions n_persons
       <int>     <int>
1         41        20
# A tibble: 1 × 1
  n_complete_persons
               <int>
1                 20
# A tibble: 2 × 3
  planned_condition n_sessions n_persons
  <fct>                  <int>     <int>
1 satiated                  21        20
2 fasted                    20        20

3 Order and carryover checks

3.1 Inspect session order structure

Check whether the order of the conditions is relevant:

  • whether participants were fasting or satiated first

  • whether order has a main effect

  • whether order moderates the effect of planned_condition

3.2 Verify crossover structure

# A tibble: 1 × 2
      n    nn
  <int> <int>
1     2    20
# A tibble: 4 × 3
  session planned_condition     n
  <fct>   <fct>             <int>
1 T1      satiated             10
2 T1      fasted               10
3 T2      satiated             11
4 T2      fasted               10

3.3 Create order variable

# A tibble: 2 × 2
  order_group        n
  <fct>          <int>
1 satiated_first    10
2 fasted_first      10

3.4 Merge order variable into the main sample

# A tibble: 1 × 3
  n_sessions n_conditions     n
       <int>        <int> <int>
1          2            2    20
# A tibble: 1 × 3
  n_rows n_ids n_missing_order
   <int> <int>           <int>
1     41    20               0
# A tibble: 4 × 4
  order_group    session planned_condition     n
  <fct>          <fct>   <fct>             <int>
1 satiated_first T1      satiated             10
2 satiated_first T2      fasted               10
3 fasted_first   T1      fasted               10
4 fasted_first   T2      satiated             11

3.5 Descriptive order check

# A tibble: 4 × 6
  order_group    planned_condition n_obs n_ids total_kcal_M total_kcal_SD
  <fct>          <fct>             <int> <int>        <dbl>         <dbl>
1 satiated_first satiated             10    10         307.          148.
2 satiated_first fasted               10    10         347.          192.
3 fasted_first   satiated             11    10         460.          153.
4 fasted_first   fasted               10    10         400.          174.

3.6 Test order effects statistically

Test three models:
A) a baseline model without order;
B) plus the main effect of order;
C) plus the interaction between planned_condition and order_group.

-> If C does not perform better than B, there is no evidence that the condition effect depends on the order.

Data: dat_main_order
Models:
m_base: total_kcal ~ planned_condition + (1 | subject)
m_order_add: total_kcal ~ planned_condition + order_group + (1 | subject)
            npar    AIC    BIC  logLik -2*log(L)  Chisq Df Pr(>Chisq)
m_base         4 524.71 531.56 -258.35    516.71                     
m_order_add    5 524.77 533.34 -257.39    514.77 1.9334  1     0.1644
Data: dat_main_order
Models:
m_order_add: total_kcal ~ planned_condition + order_group + (1 | subject)
m_order_int: total_kcal ~ planned_condition * order_group + (1 | subject)
            npar    AIC    BIC  logLik -2*log(L)  Chisq Df Pr(>Chisq)  
m_order_add    5 524.77 533.34 -257.39    514.77                       
m_order_int    6 524.02 534.30 -256.01    512.02 2.7589  1    0.09672 .
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
Linear mixed model fit by maximum likelihood . t-tests use Satterthwaite's
  method [lmerModLmerTest]
Formula: total_kcal ~ planned_condition * order_group + (1 | subject)
   Data: dat_main_order

      AIC       BIC    logLik -2*log(L)  df.resid 
    524.0     534.3    -256.0     512.0        35

     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-1.40548 -0.62621 -0.01032  0.47098  1.74286

(Intercept)                                       306.97      49.68  25.39
planned_conditionfasted                            39.96      33.77  21.14
order_groupfasted_first                           134.22      69.98  25.04
planned_conditionfasted:order_groupfasted_first   -81.02      47.36  21.17
                                                t value   Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)                                       6.180 0.00000172 ***
planned_conditionfasted                           1.183     0.2499    
order_groupfasted_first                           1.918     0.0666 .  
planned_conditionfasted:order_groupfasted_first  -1.711     0.1017

17.421

Interpretation

If anova(m_order_add, m_order_int) is not significant –> then there is no evidence that order moderates the condition effect

If anova(m_base, m_order_add) is significant –> then there is a general difference between fasted_first and satiated_first, independent of condition

4 Sample

4.1 Demographic characteristics of the final analysis sample

Characteristic N = 201
age 21.15 (2.76), [18.00, 29.00]
sex_male_m_female_f
    f 14 (70%)
    m 6 (30%)
education
    2 1 (5.0%)
    8 16 (80%)
    12 3 (15%)
1 Mean (SD), [Min, Max]; n (%)

5 Assumptions

5.1 Paired comparisons

6 Manipulation checks

6.1 Stress ratings

# A tibble: 1 × 2
  n_sessions n_ids
       <int> <int>
1         20    20
# A tibble: 1 × 8
  n_sessions n_ids baseline_M baseline_SD mild_M mild_SD strong_M strong_SD
       <int> <int>      <dbl>       <dbl>  <dbl>   <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>
1         20    20        4.2        2.12   2.86    1.90     5.14      1.57

-----------------------------
Check: Stress mild - baseline 
-----------------------------
   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
 -5.667  -1.667  -1.167  -1.342   0.125   1.000 

Shapiro Wilk Test:

    Shapiro-Wilk normality test

data:  d
W = 0.87537, p-value = 0.01462

-----------------------------
Check: Stress strong - baseline 
-----------------------------
   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
-3.0000 -0.3333  0.9167  0.9417  2.8750  5.0000 

Shapiro Wilk Test:

    Shapiro-Wilk normality test

data:  d
W = 0.96502, p-value = 0.6481

-----------------------------
Check: Stress strong - mild 
-----------------------------
   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
 -0.500   1.250   2.167   2.283   3.208   6.333 

Shapiro Wilk Test:

    Shapiro-Wilk normality test

data:  d
W = 0.96982, p-value = 0.751

    Paired t-test

data:  stress_check_session$mild_mean and stress_check_session$baseline_mean
t = -3.0217, df = 19, p-value = 0.007016
alternative hypothesis: true mean difference is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 -2.2709917 -0.4123417
sample estimates:
mean difference 
      -1.341667

data:  stress_check_session$strong_mean and stress_check_session$baseline_mean
t = 1.8547, df = 19, p-value = 0.07923
alternative hypothesis: true mean difference is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 -0.1210224  2.0043557
sample estimates:
mean difference 
      0.9416667

data:  stress_check_session$strong_mean and stress_check_session$mild_mean

alternative hypothesis: true mean difference is not equal to 0

mean difference

Check: Hunger fasted - satiated

  -1.00    1.75    3.50    3.75    6.25    8.00

data:  d
W = 0.90397, p-value = 0.04898

data:  hunger_wide$fasted and hunger_wide$satiated

alternative hypothesis: true mean difference is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 2.342881 5.157119
sample estimates:
mean difference 
           3.75

9 Model specification

9.1 Evaluate random intercept versus random slope structure

First, use a random intercept as the main model

Then test the random slope

  • Only include the random slope if the model is stable

  • no major warnings appear

  • and the fit is visibly better

9.2 Define reporting functions for mixed models

10 Main analyses

10.1 Test condition effects across outcomes

Data: dat_main_final
Models:
m0_total: total_kcal ~ order_group + (1 | subject)
m1_total: total_kcal ~ planned_condition + order_group + (1 | subject)
         npar    AIC    BIC  logLik -2*log(L) Chisq Df Pr(>Chisq)
m0_total    4 522.78 529.63 -257.39    514.78                    
m1_total    5 524.77 533.34 -257.39    514.77 0.003  1     0.9565
Data: dat_main_final
Models:
m0_high: high_kcal ~ order_group + (1 | subject)
m1_high: high_kcal ~ planned_condition + order_group + (1 | subject)
        npar    AIC    BIC  logLik -2*log(L)  Chisq Df Pr(>Chisq)
m0_high    4 494.78 501.63 -243.39    486.78                     
m1_high    5 496.07 504.64 -243.04    486.07 0.7083  1        0.4
Linear mixed model fit by maximum likelihood . t-tests use Satterthwaite's
  method [lmerModLmerTest]
Formula: high_kcal ~ planned_condition + order_group + (1 | subject)
   Data: dat_main_final

      AIC       BIC    logLik -2*log(L)  df.resid 
    496.1     504.6    -243.0     486.1        36

     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-1.54034 -0.65235  0.02507  0.55326  1.66168 

Random effects:

 subject  (Intercept) 9467     97.3    
 Residual             3170     56.3    
Number of obs: 41, groups:  subject, 20

Fixed effects:

(Intercept)               206.28      34.40  23.27   5.997 0.00000389 ***
planned_conditionfasted   -14.95      17.65  21.40  -0.847      0.406    
order_groupfasted_first    67.82      46.96  20.36   1.444      0.164

order_groupfasted_first -0.06329984 0.1174140

11 Moderation analyses

11.1 Define moderation helper functions

11.1.1 Moderation 1: SRS

m_srs_add = main effect of Condition plus main effect of Moderator

m_srs_int = plus interaction; ANOVA tests whether the interaction provides additional explanatory power

# A tibble: 1 × 6
  n_rows n_ids n_nonmiss_outcome n_nonmiss_cond n_nonmiss_mod
   <int> <int>             <int>          <int>         <int>
1     41    20                41             41            41
# ℹ 1 more variable: n_complete_for_model <int>
Data: dat_main_srs
Models:
m_srs_add: total_kcal ~ planned_condition + srs_c + order_group + (1 | subject)
m_srs_int: total_kcal ~ planned_condition * srs_c + order_group + (1 | subject)
          npar    AIC    BIC  logLik -2*log(L) Chisq Df Pr(>Chisq)
m_srs_add    6 524.95 535.23 -256.47    512.95                    
m_srs_int    7 526.40 538.40 -256.20    512.40 0.546  1       0.46

12 Sensitivity analyses

12.1 Exclude sessions with planned versus measured mismatch

What: Test robustness by excluding sessions with mismatches.

Why: This demonstrates that the main results are not driven by non-compliance. If I consider only the metabolically consistent sessions, does the main effect still hold?

# A tibble: 1 × 3
  n_rows n_ids n_missing_order
   <int> <int>           <int>
1     41    20               0
# A tibble: 2 × 4
  n_rows n_sessions n_conditions     n
   <int>      <int>        <int> <int>
1      2          2            2    18
2      3          2            2     1

Mit allen Sessions:
                         Estimate Std. Error       df     t value
(Intercept)             327.64135   48.58670 23.15165  6.74343664
planned_conditionfasted  -1.37471   25.17749 21.21167 -0.05460075
order_groupfasted_first  94.53371   66.28221 20.17624  1.42623040
                              Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept)             0.000000681124
planned_conditionfasted 0.956967547174
order_groupfasted_first 0.169092541537

Ohne mismatch Sessions:
                           Estimate Std. Error       df    t value
(Intercept)             317.6282490   52.01700 21.88705 6.10623958
planned_conditionfasted   0.1301687   26.40901 20.20511 0.00492895
order_groupfasted_first 103.8350064   69.26847 19.17987 1.49902264
                              Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept)             0.000003891543
planned_conditionfasted 0.996115624292
order_groupfasted_first 0.150147675311

12.2 Reanalyse using log transformed total energy intake

Log transformation typically:

  • makes large values less dominant

  • makes the distribution more symmetrical

  • often produces slightly cleaner residuals

What: Analyze log1p(total_kcal) as a robustness check. Does my main effect persist if I statistically reduce the skewness of the outcome distribution?

Data: dat
Models:
m0: f_m0
m1: f_m1
   npar    AIC    BIC  logLik -2*log(L)  Chisq Df Pr(>Chisq)
m0    4 50.642 57.497 -21.321    42.642                     
m1    5 52.427 60.995 -21.214    42.427 0.2151  1     0.6428
                          Estimate Std. Error       df    t value
(Intercept)              5.6629325 0.15465320 22.86751 36.6169769
planned_conditionfasted -0.0364367 0.07841066 21.05876 -0.4646906
order_groupfasted_first  0.3206490 0.21131529 20.02518  1.5173961
                                              Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept)             0.0000000000000000000008326017
planned_conditionfasted 0.6469217341118571606983778111
order_groupfasted_first 0.1447958919318401982412325424
                              2.5 %    97.5 %

.sigma                           NA        NA
